2 CONC y and Historical Reference Book of Fayette </line><line>County, Published by S. B. Nelson, Herald Press, 1922; pages 606-607:</line><line /><line>Nathan Divvens, Sr., was born in Westmoreland County, Pennsylvania, March 1, </line><line>1841, and di
2 CONC ed at Uniontown on December 23, 1898.  He was a son of the late </line><line>Jacob and Rebecca (Miller) Divvens, natives of Bedford county, Pennsylvania, </line><line>who removed to Westmoreland county, where Jacob Divvens was engaged in </line><li
2 CONC ne>agricultural pursuits until 1848, when removed with his family to Pittsburgh, </line><line>where he was engaged as a rolling mill hand until his death in 1886.  Of his </line><line>children, two sons, Nathan and Erasmus, located in Uniontown, Pe
2 CONC nnsylvania, </line><line>the former in April 1867, and the latter some years later.  </line><line /><line>Nathan Divvens attended the public schools at Pittsburgh until he had attained </line><line>his tenth year, when he began to earn his own livi
2 CONC ng.  At sixteen he was </line><line>apprenticed to learn tobacco manufacturing with Martin Heyl, Pittsburg.  Upon </line><line>completing his time, he accepted service as a journeyman with William and </line><line>David Reinhart, Pittsburgh, by who
2 CONC m he was employed until August 7, 1862, the </line><line>date of his enlistment as a private in Company I, One Hundred and Thirty-ninth </line><line>Pennsylvania Volunteer Infantry, Colonel Collier, commanding.  At the battle </line><line>of Spotsy
2 CONC lvania Court House he was wounded in the right hand by a shot or </line><line>fragment of shell which nearly severed that member from the arm, and </line><line>necessitated its amputation.  He was discharged from the service at the </line><line>hos
2 CONC pital in Pittsburgh, March 16, 1864. </line><line /><line>In the fall of the same year he established a cigar store at Sharpsburg, </line><line>Pennsylvania, removing thence in 1867, to Uniontown when he embarked in the </line><line>same business
2 CONC , establishing in connection therewith a cigar factory and adding </line><line>thereto the general tobacco dealing business in which he continued to be </line><line>engaged up to the time of his decease.  He was succeeded by his son and </line><lin
2 CONC e>namesake at the old location, No. 59 West Main Street.  </line><line /><line>Mr. Nathan Divvens, Sr., was married in 1867 to Susan E., daughter of the late </line><line>John G. Emerick, of Sharpsburg, Allegheny County, Pennsylvania.  Mr. Emeric
2 CONC k </line><line>was a school teacher at Sharpsburg, enlisted as a soldier in the war of the </line><line>Rebellion, and was killed in the engagement at Fair Oaks.  Mrs. Divvens died </line><line>July 30, 1896, leaving five children: Lily, wife of Fr
2 CONC ank Lewellen, of </line><line>Uniontown; Nathan, who succeeded his father in business; Pearl, wife of Robert </line><line>E. Saul, New York City; Herbert, who is in the employ of his brother; and Miss </line><line>Edna Divvens.  Mr. Divvens was a m
2 CONC ember of the Lutheran church and of the </line><line>Order of the Knights of Pythias. </line><line /><line>Additional Comments:</line><line>Nathan's mother was Elizabeth Rebecca Miller.</line><line /><line>This file has been created by a form at ht

2 CONC n July 17, 1936. Mr. Staylor preceded his wife in death.&lt;/p&gt;&lt;p&gt;Surviving are the following children: William W.W. Staylor, Jr. of Cumberland, Md.; Richard R. R. Staylor of Warner Robings, Ga.; Marjorie M. M. Staylor of Johnstown and Cha
